name: Cape Hunting Dog
tagline: The most successful pack hunters in Africa—they almost never miss!
intro: You could get eaten alive by a pack of Cape hunting dogs—if you were, say, an antelope. These dogs (also called African hunting dogs) kill animals much larger than themselves by using spectacular teamwork. During the long chase usually required to bring down their prey, the dogs signal to each other with their white tail-tips. One dog finally grabs the exhausted animal's nose, another its tail, and the rest snap at its middle. Luckily, these wild dogs never hunt people.

facts.label: How it kills
facts.value: Kills by group attack, by biting its prey

facts.label: Watch out
facts.value: Cape hunting dogs have been found at altitudes of over 5,600 meters (18,480 ft) on the snowy slopes of Mount Kilimanjaro.

topics.title: Skeleton story
topics.text: A skeleton's design can give you clues about what its owner did for a living. The strong jaws and sharp, pointed teeth of these skeletons tell you that the animals killed and ate other animals. The long legs tell you that the animals could cover great distances. If you guess that these are the skeletons of powerful canine hunters, you're right.

topics.title: Potential prey
topics.text: When Cape hunting dogs are prowling the African savanna, the grazing animals need to be constantly on the lookout.

topics.title: The competition
topics.text: Which of these African carnivores do you think could really ruin a hunting dog's day?

captions.label: Cheetah
captions.text: A cheetah can outsprint a hunting dog, but the dog would win in a long-distance race.

captions.label: Lion
captions.text: Hungry lions sometimes steal a kill made by hunting dogs.

captions.label: Spotted hyena
captions.text: This is a Cape hunting dog's worst rival. Hyenas frequently steal the dogs' kills.

captions.label: Leopard
captions.text: A leopard would be glad to get its claws on an unprotected pup.

topics.title: Wild canines
topics.text: Members of the dog, or canine, family exist in nearly every country. The ones shown here are not "man's best friend" but wild animals.

captions.label: Maned wolf
captions.text: You see this canine only on the plains of South America.

captions.label: Fennec fox
captions.text: This African desert dweller uses its big ears to shed body heat and locate prey at night.

captions.label: Australian dingo
captions.text: The ancestors of dingos were partially tamed dogs brought to Australia by people. Those ancient dogs left their human friends thousands of years ago for life in the wild.

topics.title: Greeting ceremony
topics.text: In hunting-dog packs, there's a strict hierarchy—that is, everyone knows who's got the power. When two dogs greet each other, each one signals that it recognizes the other's position in the pack. The leaders are the only ones who get to have pups, but any grown-up is happy to babysit.

hotspots.label: One for all and all for one

_provenance.note: Text liberated from original screen art; images & clip restored from disc. Original media is Microsoft/supplier copyright — placeholder pending swap to open-licensed assets.

video.title: One for all and all for one
video.caption: A Cape hunting dog is not a very big animal. And it doesn't have claws or poison that it can use to wound its prey. To be well-fed carnivores on the African savanna, hunting dogs must live and hunt in packs.
